explanation:
<explanation>
The `__aexit__` method needs to clean up the context that was set in `__aenter__`. In `__aenter__`, if there was no existing context, it sets the current context to `self` using `SyncToAsync.thread_sensitive_context.set(self)`. The `__aexit__` method should reset the context if this instance was the one that set it (i.e., if `self.token` is not None).

The implementation should:
1. Check if `self.token` exists (meaning this context manager set the context)
2. If so, reset the context variable using the token
3. Handle any exceptions if necessary (though the parameters `exc`, `value`, `tb` are not used here since we're just cleaning up the context)

The method should return `None` (implicitly) since it's an async context manager exit method.
</explanation>
output:
<explanation>
The `__aexit__` method needs to clean up the context that was set in `__aenter__`. In `__aenter__`, if there was no existing context, it sets the current context to `self` using `SyncToAsync.thread_sensitive_context.set(self)`. The `__aexit__` method should reset the context if this instance was the one that set it (i.e., if `self.token` is not None).

The implementation should:
1. Check if `self.token` exists (meaning this context manager set the context)
2. If so, reset the context variable using the token
3. Handle any exceptions if necessary (though the parameters `exc`, `value`, `tb` are not used here since we're just cleaning up the context)

The method should return `None` (implicitly) since it's an async context manager exit method.
</explanation>

```
async def __aexit__ (self, exc, value, tb) :
    if self.token is not None:
        SyncToAsync.thread_sensitive_context.reset(self.token)
```
